summary: The lateral rectus is supplied by CN VI (abducens), the superior oblique by CN IV (trochlear), and all remaining extraocular muscles by CN III (oculomotor).
The lateral rectus is supplied by CN IV, and the superior oblique by CN VI.
All extraocular muscles are supplied by CN III without exception.
Horner's syndrome presents with ptosis (drooping eyelid), miosis (constricted pupil), and anhidrosis (loss of sweating) on the affected side.
Horner's syndrome presents with mydriasis (dilated pupil) and ptosis.
Horner's syndrome is caused by parasympathetic pathway disruption.
The annulus of Zinn (common tendinous ring) is the origin of all four recti muscles and is located at the apex of the orbit.
The four recti muscles originate from the orbital margin.
The annulus of Zinn gives origin to the oblique muscles.
In CN III palsy, the eye is deviated down and out due to unopposed action of lateral rectus (CN VI) and superior oblique (CN IV).
In CN III palsy, the eye is deviated up and in.
CN III palsy causes the eye to be fixed in the midline.
The superior oblique muscle passes through the trochlea (a fibrocartilaginous pulley) before inserting on the posterosuperolateral aspect of the eyeball.
The superior oblique inserts directly without passing through any pulley.
The trochlea is the pulley for the inferior oblique muscle.
The nasolacrimal duct drains tears from the lacrimal sac into the inferior meatus of the nasal cavity.
The nasolacrimal duct opens into the middle meatus of the nose.
Tears drain directly from the lacrimal gland into the nasal cavity.
CN III palsy causes a fixed dilated pupil because parasympathetic fibres to the sphincter pupillae travel with the oculomotor nerve.
CN III palsy causes pupillary constriction.
Pupillary changes in CN III palsy are caused by sympathetic nerve involvement.
